content.text: HTTP callbacks — commonly called webhooks — let a remote system push event notifications directly to your application the moment something happens, eliminating the need for repeated polling. They underpin nearly every integration between modern SaaS platforms and agent backends.
A webhook is a user-defined HTTP callback. A source system (GitHub, Stripe, Shopify, an LLM API) records a URL you provide, and whenever a configured event occurs it makes an outbound
request to that URL with a JSON (or form-encoded) payload describing the event. Your server receives the request, processes it, and returns a

Unlike polling — where your code repeatedly asks "has anything happened?" — webhooks invert the communication: the provider asks you. This push model means your service is idle until work actually arrives, and events are processed in near-real-time rather than on a polling interval.

In agentic systems webhooks are especially valuable because agents often orchestrate long-running tasks across external services. A payment agent might register a Stripe webhook to learn when a charge succeeds; a code-review agent registers a GitHub webhook to be notified of new pull requests; an order-fulfillment agent listens for Shopify order events. Without webhooks, the agent would have to poll every provider on a timer, which wastes resources and adds latency.

Polling introduces three compounding problems. First, latency: if you poll every 30 seconds an event that just happened won't be processed for up to 30 seconds. Second, wasted requests: most polls return "nothing new", consuming API-rate-limit budget and network bandwidth for no value. Third, scale: at 1,000 event sources you must maintain 1,000 polling loops, each with its own connection, cursor, and error-handling logic.

Webhooks solve all three: delivery is immediate (sub-second from event occurrence to your handler), your server is silent until an event arrives, and one HTTP endpoint can receive events from arbitrarily many sources. For agents that orchestrate payments, code pipelines, or customer-data workflows, near-real-time reaction is the difference between a usable product and a frustrating one.
— events reach your handler within milliseconds of occurring, not after a polling delay.
— your server does no work between events; CPU and memory consumption are proportional to actual event volume.
— works through load balancers, API gateways, WAFs, and TLS-terminating proxies with no special protocol.
— the provider and your agent don't share a process, queue, or database; they communicate only over HTTP.
— reputable providers retry failed deliveries with exponential back-off and dead-letter queues, giving you at-least-once delivery guarantees.
— a single source event can be delivered to multiple subscriber URLs simultaneously, enabling independent downstream consumers.
— local development is painful; you need a tunnel (ngrok, cloudflared) or a staging environment to receive live events.
— retries mean your handler may receive the same event multiple times; idempotency logic is mandatory, not optional.
— if a retry arrives after the next successful event, your handler sees events out of sequence. Providers often include a sequence number or timestamp; you must check it.
— if the provider's outbound delivery fails silently (a bug on their side) you may miss events entirely. Complement webhooks with periodic reconciliation queries.
— providers typically wait only 5-30 seconds for a 2xx response before marking delivery as failed. Long-running processing must be deferred to a background queue.
— a burst of events will hammer your endpoint. Rate limiting and queue buffers on your side are necessary at scale.
The lifecycle of a webhook event has four phases: registration, dispatch, acknowledgement, and retry.

you tell the provider which events you care about and supply a URL. The provider stores your subscription. Modern providers (GitHub, Stripe) allow registration via API rather than only the dashboard, enabling programmatic subscription management.
When the configured event occurs, the provider constructs a JSON payload, optionally computes an HMAC signature over the payload body using a shared secret, adds it as a request header (e.g.
), and makes an HTTP POST to your registered URL. This is the
Your endpoint verifies the signature, enqueues the event for background processing, and immediately returns
tells the provider delivery succeeded. Any non-2xx response, or a timeout, triggers the retry logic.

schedule typically follows exponential back-off: attempt 1 is immediate, attempt 2 after 30 seconds, attempt 3 after 5 minutes, and so on, up to a ceiling (often 24-72 hours). After all retries are exhausted, the provider may send an alert or move the event to a dead-letter log. The HMAC signature on a retry is identical to the original, so your idempotency check on the event ID handles duplicates transparently.
The signature scheme ensures authenticity. The provider and you share a secret
and sends it in a header. Your endpoint recomputes the same HMAC and compares with
to avoid timing attacks. An attacker who can forge a POST but does not know

The diagram below shows the complete webhook flow: event generation, HTTP POST delivery, fan-out to multiple subscribers, exponential retry on failure, and the three-step signature verification process.

Think of webhooks as a doorbell, not a peephole. Polling is like walking to the window every 30 seconds to check whether a delivery van has arrived. A webhook is the doorbell: the delivery driver presses it the moment they arrive. You respond immediately, sign for the package, and go back to what you were doing. The doorbell analogy also captures the failure mode: if you're asleep (endpoint down) the driver may try again later, but if you never answer the package eventually goes back to the depot (dead-letter queue).

Another way to frame it: webhooks invert the dependency arrow. With polling your service depends on the remote service's read API. With webhooks, the remote service depends on your write endpoint. This inversion is powerful for decoupling and for keeping your API-rate-limit budget free for queries that actually need answers.
Webhooks are the right choice when:
You need near-real-time reaction to external events (payment charged, pull request opened, order shipped).
The source system is a third-party SaaS that offers a webhook subscription API (GitHub, Stripe, Twilio, Slack, Shopify, SendGrid).
Event volume is moderate and bursty — webhooks handle spikes well because the provider queues retries, not your client.
Your agent must trigger side effects or downstream work immediately after an event (send a notification, update a database, kick off a pipeline).
Prefer polling or a message queue instead when:
The source system doesn't support webhooks and you must query a REST API on a schedule.
You need guaranteed ordering across multiple event types — webhooks don't provide cross-event sequencing.
You're building internal service-to-service communication — use a message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ) for higher throughput and richer delivery semantics.
The consumer is behind a firewall or in a non-public network where the provider cannot reach it.
A production-quality webhook endpoint follows four implementation rules:
Verify the signature before processing any payload.
Respond HTTP 200 immediately — defer all real work to a background queue.
Deduplicate by event ID — store processed IDs and skip re-deliveries.
Log the raw payload before parsing — makes debugging provider bugs much easier.

# webhook_receiver.py — FastAPI webhook endpoint with signature verification
import hashlib
import hmac
import json
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException, BackgroundTasks
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
import logging

app = FastAPI()
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

WEBHOOK_SECRET = b"your-shared-secret-here"  # store in env var

# In-memory dedup set (use Redis in production)
processed_event_ids: set[str] = set()

def verify_github_signature(payload_body: bytes, signature_header: str) -> bool:
    """Verify HMAC-SHA256 signature from GitHub."""
    if not signature_header or not signature_header.startswith("sha256="):
        return False
    expected = "sha256=" + hmac.new(
        WEBHOOK_SECRET, payload_body, hashlib.sha256
    ).hexdigest()
    # constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks
    return hmac.compare_digest(expected, signature_header)

def process_event_background(event_type: str, payload: dict) -> None:
    """Heavy work runs here, outside the HTTP response window."""
    logger.info("Processing event=%s id=%s", event_type, payload.get("id"))
    # ... actual business logic ...

@app.post("/webhook/github")
async def github_webhook(
    request: Request,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
):
    payload_bytes = await request.body()
    signature = request.headers.get("X-Hub-Signature-256", "")
    event_type = request.headers.get("X-GitHub-Event", "unknown")
    delivery_id = request.headers.get("X-GitHub-Delivery", "")

    # 1. Verify signature
    if not verify_github_signature(payload_bytes, signature):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid signature")

    # 2. Deduplicate
    if delivery_id in processed_event_ids:
        return JSONResponse({"status": "duplicate, skipped"})
    processed_event_ids.add(delivery_id)

    # 3. Log raw payload
    logger.debug("raw_payload=%s", payload_bytes[:500])

    # 4. Parse and dispatch asynchronously
    payload = json.loads(payload_bytes)
    background_tasks.add_task(process_event_background, event_type, payload)

    # 5. Acknowledge immediately — must return 200 fast
    return JSONResponse({"status": "accepted"}, status_code=200)
Simple example: registering a GitHub webhook via API

import requests

GITHUB_TOKEN = "ghp_..."
REPO = "owner/repo"
WEBHOOK_URL = "https://your-app.example.com/webhook/github"
SECRET = "randomly-generated-secret-32-chars"

response = requests.post(
    f"https://api.github.com/repos/{REPO}/hooks",
    headers={
        "Authorization": f"token {GITHUB_TOKEN}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
    },
    json={
        "name": "web",
        "active": True,
        "events": ["push", "pull_request", "issues"],
        "config": {
            "url": WEBHOOK_URL,
            "content_type": "json",
            "secret": SECRET,
            "insecure_ssl": "0",
        },
    },
)
print(response.status_code, response.json().get("id"))
Realistic example: Stripe payment webhook with queue

A CI/CD orchestration agent at a mid-size engineering org handles 2,000 GitHub events per day. Every push and pull_request event triggers the agent to run lint, test, and security scans. The architecture works as follows:
A load-balanced FastAPI service behind an HTTPS terminator receives GitHub webhook POSTs. It verifies signatures, deduplicates by
header, and publishes to a Kafka topic within 20ms.
Three Kafka consumers — lint worker, test worker, security scanner — each consume independently from the same topic, allowing parallel execution without shared state.
After completing their tasks, workers write GitHub Check results back via the Checks API, giving developers instant inline feedback in pull requests.
A separate reconciliation cron job runs every 6 hours, querying the GitHub API for any PRs whose Check status is missing, compensating for any webhook events that were silently dropped.

Key lessons from this deployment: the reconciliation job is non-optional — over a 30-day period it recovered 12 events that GitHub failed to deliver. The Kafka topic acts as a buffer absorbing merge-queue bursts that would otherwise overwhelm downstream workers.

The most common mistake is running the full business logic inside the HTTP handler before returning a response. If the logic takes more than a few seconds, the provider times out and retries — causing duplicate processing and cascading failures.

# WRONG — blocks the HTTP response, triggers retry storms
@app.post("/webhook")
async def bad_webhook(request: Request):
    data = await request.json()
    send_email(data["customer"])   # takes 3 seconds
    update_database(data)           # takes 1 second
    call_external_api(data)         # takes 2 seconds
    return {"status": "done"}       # 6s total — provider already gave up!

# CORRECT — acknowledge immediately, work in background
@app.post("/webhook")
async def good_webhook(request: Request, bg: BackgroundTasks):
    data = await request.json()
    bg.add_task(process_event, data)
    return {"status": "accepted"}   # returns in < 50ms

Omitting HMAC verification means any attacker who knows your endpoint URL can forge arbitrary webhook payloads, triggering payments, order cancellations, or privilege escalations. Always verify before parsing the body.

At-least-once delivery is a guarantee, not a failure mode. If your handler charges a customer or creates a database row on every call, re-deliveries will create duplicate charges and rows. Store processed event IDs in Redis or a dedupe table and check before acting.

HMAC verification requires the raw bytes. If a framework middleware has already parsed the body into a JSON dict by the time your route handler runs, the raw bytes are gone and the HMAC will fail. Always read the raw body for signature verification, then parse it separately.
Use a dedicated URL per event source
) so logging, rate limiting, and secret rotation are independent.
— most providers allow updating the secret without re-creating the subscription. Keep the old and new secret active for a brief overlap window during rotation.
— Stripe includes the event timestamp in the signed payload. Reject any event older than 5 minutes to prevent replay attacks where an attacker captures and replays a valid signed request.
Emit structured logs with event_id, event_type, duration, and status
— makes debugging provider delivery failures trivial.
— query the provider's events API for anything your webhook missed. Even the most reliable providers have had outages that silently dropped deliveries.
Use a tunnel for local development
forward live webhook traffic to your local server, so you can iterate without deploying.
A webhook endpoint has two performance targets: P99 latency under 500ms (to stay well within most providers' 30s timeout) and throughput matching peak event rate without back-pressure building up.

Because the handler does almost nothing — verify, deduplicate, enqueue, respond — a single async Python worker can handle roughly 1,000 requests per second with sub-10ms P99 latency (measured on a 2-vCPU instance). The bottleneck is rarely the HTTP server; it's the Redis write for idempotency tracking. Use a pipelined Redis command or a Lua script to make the dedup-and-enqueue atomic and fast.

For bursty traffic (e.g., a Shopify flash sale that generates 50,000 order events in 90 seconds), horizontal scaling is trivial because the handler is stateless — add more instances behind a load balancer. The queue (Redis or Kafka) absorbs the burst; consumers drain at their own pace. The key formula for sizing the queue depth is:

For a peak rate of 600 events/second and a tolerated lag of 60 seconds, provision at minimum 36,000 queue slots. The handler itself should be measured with a load test tool (e.g. Locust or k6) simulating 10x expected peak before going live.
gives you a public HTTPS URL in seconds. The web inspector at
shows every request and lets you replay them.
forwards live events and handles signature injection automatically.
— Cloudflare's tunnel tool, useful when you're already in a Cloudflare-secured infrastructure.
— if you're the webhook sender (not receiver), Svix provides a hosted delivery engine with retries, fan-out, and a subscriber portal.
— proxy that sits between any provider and your server, normalizing payloads, adding retries, and providing a debugging dashboard.
— event-driven workflow platform where webhooks are first-class triggers for durable functions.
— use FastAPI's test client to POST synthetic payloads with crafted HMAC signatures to unit-test your handler.
sends a real signed event to your local listener for integration testing.
Every major provider uses HMAC-SHA256 but names the header differently: GitHub uses
field (signed payload includes the timestamp to prevent replays); Shopify uses
to prevent timing side-channels, even for hex strings.

An attacker who captures a valid signed request can replay it indefinitely if you only check the signature. Stripe embeds a Unix timestamp in the signed string; reject any event with a timestamp more than 5 minutes old. For providers that don't embed a timestamp, combine your own event-ID deduplication with short TTL caching: a 5-minute Redis TTL on seen event IDs closes the replay window.
Return 200 even for provider configuration errors
— if a payload has an unexpected shape, log it and acknowledge; don't return 400, which triggers the provider's retry loop for a non-recoverable error.
Use exactly-once semantics at the database layer
— a unique constraint on the event ID column is more reliable than an in-memory set, which is lost on restart.
— GitHub, Stripe, and Shopify all expose webhook delivery logs in their dashboards and APIs. Alert on sustained failure rates above 1%.
Set up a dead-letter alerting path
— if a provider exhausts all retries, you need a page-alert and a manual replay tool. Design for this failure mode before it happens.
Webhooks push events to your endpoint via HTTP POST the moment they occur, eliminating polling latency and wasted API calls.
Always verify the HMAC-SHA256 signature using
Acknowledge with HTTP 200 immediately and defer all processing to a background queue; never do slow work inline.
Idempotency is mandatory: providers retry on failure, so your handler must handle duplicate deliveries safely by checking event IDs.
Complement webhooks with periodic reconciliation queries against the provider's API to recover any silently dropped events.
For high-volume or bursty traffic, route events through a message queue (Redis list, Kafka) between the HTTP handler and consumers to absorb spikes without blocking acknowledgement.
Use tunnels (ngrok, Stripe CLI) for local development; use managed platforms (Svix, Hookdeck) when you need delivery reliability without building it yourself.
